---
title: "Decision Trees and Random Forest"
author: 
  - "Grace Stewart"
  - "DS303, SP26"
  - "Prof. Amber Camp"
date: 4/13/26
format: html
editor: visual
theme: spacelab
---

## Where We Are

All semester, we've been working along a spectrum:

> **EXPLAIN ←————————————————————→ PREDICT**
>
> Linear Regression → Logistic Regression → Mixed Effects → **Decision Trees → Random Forest**

Regression models are powerful because they're *interpretable*: we can say "for every one-unit increase in X, Y changes by β." But they make assumptions: linearity, normality of residuals, independence.

Today we move toward the **predict** end of the spectrum. Decision trees and random forests make *fewer* assumptions about the shape of the data, handle nonlinear relationships naturally, and often produce excellent predictions. The tradeoff: they're harder to interpret. This is not a bug, and it can be a useful design choice you make deliberately.

## Part 1: Decision Trees

### The Core Idea

A decision tree asks a sequence of yes/no questions about your predictors, splitting the data at each step to reduce error. It looks like a flowchart. Each split is chosen to make the resulting groups as *pure* (homogeneous) as possible.

For classification: we want splits that put mostly one class in each group.\
For regression: we want splits that minimize variance within each group.

The tree keeps splitting until it hits a stopping rule (minimum node size, maximum depth, etc.).

### Packages

We'll use two new packages today:

-   `rpart`: builds decision trees (regression and classification)
-   `rpart.plot`: visualizes them cleanly
-   `randomForest`: builds random forests

```{r setup}

# install.packages(c("rpart", "rpart.plot", "randomForest")) # install as needed

library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
library(randomForest)
library(tidyverse) # we will used ggplot2 and dplyr mostly today
```

### Dataset: iris

We'll use the built-in `iris` dataset: 150 flowers, 4 measurements, 3 species. It's a classic classification problem.

```{r iris-data}

glimpse(iris)
head(iris)

ggplot(iris, aes(Petal.Length, Petal.Width, color = Species)) +
  geom_point()
```

**Goal today:** predict `Species` from petal and sepal measurements.

### Train/Test Split

Before building any model, we split into training (70%) and test (30%) sets. This is critical so that we can evaluate on data the model has *never seen*

```{r train-test-split}

set.seed(42)

n <- nrow(iris)
train_idx <- sample(1:n, size = floor(0.7 * n))

train <- iris[train_idx, ]
test  <- iris[-train_idx, ]
```

### Build the Tree

```{r build-tree}

tree_model <- rpart(
  Species ~ .,        # predict Species from all other variables
  data = train,
  method = "class")    # use "class" for classification, "anova" for regression

summary(tree_model)
```

### Visualize the Tree

```{r plot-tree}
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

rpart.plot(
  tree_model,
  type = 4,
  extra = 104,         # show class probabilities and % of observations
  fallen.leaves = TRUE,
  main = "Decision Tree: Iris Species")

```

How to read this: Each node shows the predicted class, the class probabilities, and the percentage of observations in that node. Follow the branches based on the condition at each split

::: callout-note
## What's is the model doing?

Notice that it splits first on `Petal.Length`. This is the variable that most cleanly separates the species. The tree found this automatically! We didn't tell it which predictor to use first.
:::

### Evaluate on Test Data

```{r tree-predictions}

tree_preds <- predict(tree_model, newdata = test, type = "class")

# confusion matrix
confusion <- table(Predicted = tree_preds, Actual = test$Species)
confusion

# accuracy
accuracy <- sum(diag(confusion)) / sum(confusion)
cat("Test Accuracy:", round(accuracy, 3), "\n")
```

Interpretation**:** The diagonal of the confusion matrix shows correct predictions. Off-diagonal entries are misclassifications.

## Part 2: The Problem with One Tree

Decision trees have a well-known weakness: **high variance**. Change your training data slightly and you can get a very different tree. This makes single trees somewhat unstable

The solution? Don't use one tree. Use *hundreds* of them.

This is the intuition behind [**bagging**]{.underline} (Bootstrap AGGregating):

1.  Take many bootstrap samples of your training data (sample *with replacement*)
2.  Train a tree on each sample
3.  Average (or vote on) their predictions

Bagging reduces variance significantly. Random forest takes this one step further

## Part 3: Random Forests

### The Main Difference

Random forests add one more trick on top of bagging: **random feature selection at each split!**

When building each tree, at every split the model only considers a *random subset* of predictors, not all of them. This forces diversity among the trees, so they don't all end up looking similar (which would defeat the purpose of averaging)

The result: a collection of de-correlated trees whose aggregate predictions are much more stable and accurate than any single tree.

### The (simplified) Algorithm

```         
for i in 1 to n_trees:
    1. Draw a bootstrap sample from the training data
    2. Grow a tree:
       - At each split, consider only m_try random features
       - Pick the best split among those m_try features
       - Split the node
       - Repeat until stopping rule
    3. Add tree to the forest

Final prediction: majority vote (classification) or average (regression)
```

The key parameter: **`mtry`**

-   how many features to consider at each split

-   Classification default: `√p` (where p = number of features) - Regression default: `p/3`

### Build the Random Forest

```{r build-rf}

set.seed(42)

rf_model <- randomForest(
  Species ~ .,
  data = train,
  ntree = 50,       # number of trees (more = more stable, slower)
  mtry = 2,         # features considered at each split (default for classing: sqrt(4) ≈ 2)
  importance = TRUE)  # compute variable importance

rf_model
```

::: callout-note
## OOB Error

The **Out-Of-Bag (OOB) error** is a built-in estimate of test error. Because each tree is trained on a bootstrap sample (\~63% of the data), the remaining \~37% can be used to evaluate it "for free", without a separate validation set. This is one of the practical advantages of random forests :)
:::

### Error Rate by Number of Trees

```{r rf-error-plot}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

plot(rf_model, main = "OOB Error Rate by Number of Trees")
legend("topright",legend = colnames(rf_model$err.rate), col = 1:4, lty = 1, cex = 0.8)
```

The error rate stabilizes after a few hundred trees. Adding more trees beyond that gives diminishing returns.

### Evaluate on Test Data

```{r rf-predictions}

rf_preds <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test)

rf_confusion <- table(Predicted = rf_preds, Actual = test$Species)
rf_confusion

rf_accuracy <- sum(diag(rf_confusion)) / sum(rf_confusion)
cat("Random Forest Test Accuracy:", round(rf_accuracy, 3), "\n")
```

### Compare: Single Tree vs. Random Forest

```{r compare}

cat("Single Decision Tree Accuracy: ", round(accuracy, 3), "\n")
cat("Random Forest Accuracy:        ", round(rf_accuracy, 3), "\n")
```

The random forest will typically match or exceed the single tree, especially on noisier data. On a clean dataset like `iris`, the difference may be small or negligible. That's to be expected for this "teaching" data

## Part 4: Variable Importance

One way to partially "look inside" a random forest is **variable importance**. Which features did the model rely on most?

```{r variable-importance}
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

varImpPlot(
  rf_model,
  main = "Variable Importance: Iris Random Forest",
  type = 1)  # Mean Decrease in Accuracy (permutation-based)

```

Mean Decrease in Accuracy: For each variable, the model randomly shuffles that variable's values and measures how much prediction accuracy drops. A large drop = that variable matters a lot

::: callout-note
## This is not the same as a regression coefficient!

A coefficient tells you *direction* and *magnitude* of an effect. Variable importance tells you *how much the model relies on that variable*. You can't say "for every 1 cm increase in `Petal.Length`, the probability of `setosa` increases by X." We gave up that interpretive power :(
:::

## Summary: When to Use What

| Situation | Reach for... |
|----|----|
| You need to explain *why* X affects Y | Regression (linear, logistic, mixed) |
| You need the best possible prediction | Random forest |
| Your data has nonlinear relationships | Random forest |
| You need to publish statistical findings | Regression |
| You have a small dataset | Be cautious with random forest |
| You want to know which variables matter | Both work (different methods) |

## Things to think about

Before you leave, think about a dataset you care about:

1.  Is your research question more "explain" or "predict"?
2.  Would a decision tree or random forest be appropriate? Why or why not?
3.  What would you *lose* by using a random forest instead of logistic regression on your data?

## Going Further (Optional)

If you want to go deeper on your own or for future projects:

-   `ranger` package: a faster C++ implementation of random forests, preferred for larger datasets
-   Gradient boosting (XGBoost, lightGBM): another ensemble method, often even more accurate
-   SHAP values: a technique for interpreting black-box models by measuring each feature's contribution to individual predictions
-   `tidymodels`: a modern R framework for machine learning workflows that works with many model types (Amber doesn't use this, finds it cumbersome, but maybe you'll like it!)
